coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of multicolor luminescent carbon dots based on carboxymethyl chitosan for cell imaging and wound healing application: In vitro and in vivo studies
coredata.dc:creator: Zong, Mingrui
Zhang, Zheyuan
Ning, Xiao
Cheng, Huaiyi
Zhao, Yifan
Ren, Jianing
Liu, Yingyu
Zhang, Ran
Cui, Jiayu
Hou, Yuxi
Li, Bing
Wu, Xiuping
coredata.dc:description: The construction of biomaterials that can facilitate wound healing is significantly challenging in the medical field, and bacterial infections increase this complexity. In this study, we selected the biomacromolecule carboxymethyl chitosan as a carbon source and citric acid as an auxiliary carbon source. We prepared carbon quantum dots with multicolor luminescence properties and higher quantum yields (QYs) using a facile one-pot hydrothermal method. We characterized them to select carbon dots (CDs) suitable for cell growth. Subsequently, their biocompatibility with L929 cells, antibacterial properties against Staphylococcus aureus, and efficiency in promoting wound healing in vivo were investigated. Our experimental results showed that CDs at an appropriate concentration had excellent bioimaging ability, were suitable for cell growth, and accelerated the healing of infected wounds. We believe these bioactive CDs have great potential in promoting wound healing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescent
Carboxymethyl chitosan
Biocompatibility
Antibacterial
Wound healing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Biocompatibility and cytofluorimetric imaging of CDs
Live/dead cell viability assay of L929
In vivo wound healing evaluation and histology staining
Effect of Mg-CDs on the proliferation of L929
In vitro antibacterial performance of CDs
Wound antibacterial and healing efficacy of CDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was funded by the Shanxi Province Science and Technology Innovation Key Talent Team ( 202204051002034 ), the Key Research and Development Plan of Shanxi Province ( 202102130501002 ), the key country-specific science and cooperation projects of the Shanxi Provincial Science and Technology Department ( 202204041101004 ), and the central guidance for local science and technology development funds ( YDZJSX2022C029 and YDZJSX2021C011 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) observed under UV excitation and visible light a) pH 6–7 b) pH 5–6 c) pH 4–5 d) pH 3–4. e) UV–vis absorption, maximum PL excitation, and emission spectrum of CDs. f) CDs under different wavelength excitation. g–i) Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the CDs, the insets are the size distribution, HRTEM. j) XRD pattern of CDs. k) Zeta Potential of CDs.
a) Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectrum of the carbon dots (CDs). b) XPS analysis of carbon dots (CDs) in full spectrum. High-resolution c-e) C1s, N1s, and O1s.

a) In vitro cell proliferation on day 1 in culture conditions of carbon dots (CDs) as determined by CCK-8. b) The proliferation of L929 cells in different carbon dot (CD) concentrations. c) Representative CLSM images for a live/dead cell viability assay of L929 using different carbon dots (CDs) concentrations. Scale bars, 100 μm (*p < 0.05 and **p < 0.01).

Cell imaging at 40× magnification a-e) under 100 μg·mL−1 culture conditions. f-j) under 200 μg·mL−1 culture conditions. a, f) 405 nm excitation, b, g) 488 nm excitation, c, h) 561 nm excitation. Scale bars, 50 μm.

a) Digital photographs of S. aureus colonies in agar plates. b) Corresponding bacterial viability measurement of S. aureus. c) Fluorescence imaging of bacteria. Scale bars, 50 μm. d) Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images of CDs adsorbed on the surface of bacteria. Red circles represent the typical areas. Scale bars, 1 μm. e) SEM images of S. aureus with different treatments. Orange arrows represent the typical distortion of bacterial morphology. Scale bars, 1 μm. f) Antibacterial mechanism.

a) Relative mean wound area of S. aureus-infected mice 14 days post-treatment. b) Representative macroscopic digital images of S. aureus-infected wounds. c) Representative photos of hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining of dermal wounds. Yellow arrows represent inflammatory cells, including lymphocytes, neutrophils, and macrophages; black arrows reflect hair follicles; blue arrows indicate fibroblasts; and red arrows indicate new blood vessels. Scale bars, 200 μm. Scale bars for the magnified images, 50 μm. d) Micrographs of immunohistochemical staining of IL-6 and TGF-β in granulation tissues. Scale bars, 50 μm. e) Quantification of immunohistochemical positive areas (*p < 0.05, **p < 0.01 and *** p < 0.001).
Schematic diagram of multicolor luminescent carbon dots (CDs) synthesis using the one-step hydrothermal method. b) With urea. c) Without urea

head.ce:title: Synthesis of multicolor luminescent carbon dots based on carboxymethyl chitosan for cell imaging and wound healing application: In vitro and in vivo studies

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Shanxi Medical University School and Hospital of Stomatology, Shanxi Province Key Laboratory of Oral Diseases Prevention and New Material, Taiyuan 030001, Shanxi, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanxi Medical University School and Hospital of Stomatology
Shanxi Province Key Laboratory of Oral Diseases Prevention and New Material

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The construction of biomaterials that can facilitate wound healing is significantly challenging in the medical field, and bacterial infections increase this complexity. In this study, we selected the biomacromolecule carboxymethyl chitosan as a carbon source and citric acid as an auxiliary carbon source. We prepared carbon quantum dots with multicolor luminescence properties and higher quantum yields (QYs) using a facile one-pot hydrothermal method. We characterized them to select carbon dots (CDs) suitable for cell growth. Subsequently, their biocompatibility with L929 cells, antibacterial properties against Staphylococcus aureus, and efficiency in promoting wound healing in vivo were investigated. Our experimental results showed that CDs at an appropriate concentration had excellent bioimaging ability, were suitable for cell growth, and accelerated the healing of infected wounds. We believe these bioactive CDs have great potential in promoting wound healing.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Oral and maxillofacial trauma, caused by infections, injuries, and tumors, has become a common clinical problem in the field of stomatology over recent years [1]. It not only affects chewing, swallowing, and pronunciation but also causes facial deformity in severe cases, affecting an individual's physical and mental health and quality of life [2,3]. The wound healing process in maxillofacial skin is susceptible to several negative factors, such as necrotic tissue, pathogen infection, diabetes, and obesity, which prolong repair time at each stage and may eventually affect the wound tissue structure or function. Among these negative factors, Staphylococcus aureus infection is the most common and major factor [4]. Traditional gauze-based dressings may prevent bleeding from the wound surface and are easy to manufacture and use. However, traditional dressings are less effective regarding hemostasis and antibacterial properties, and granulation tissue tends to grow into the material, leading to skin adhesion to the material and crusting. In addition, these dressings provide only passive protection and do not actively promote wound healing [5]. Therefore, finding a material with efficient moisturizing effects that promotes wound healing is crucial. With the continuous development in nanotechnology, nanomaterials have shown increasingly important application potential in various fields, including in the diagnosis and treatment of diseases, molecular probe generation, preparation of 3D nanostructures, slow- and controlled-release formulations, and targeted drug delivery [6,7].

Carbon dots (CDs), spherical nanoparticles, have recently gained significant attention because of their good biocompatibility, fluorescence characteristics, and low toxicity [8–10]. These advantages provide potential prospects for their application in bioimaging [11] and regenerative medicine [12]. Microorganisms may absorb and modify CDs easily; therefore, they have been widely used in antimicrobial therapy and drug delivery systems in recent years [13]. Furthermore, the synthesis of CDs is considerably simple—any organic matter containing elemental carbon can be synthesized into CDs.

Carboxymethyl chitosan (CMC), a chitosan derivative, is a carbon-containing organic matter. It has excellent water-soluble, film-forming, and slow-release properties and exhibits broad application prospects in tissue regeneration [14]. CMC differs from chitosan because it has a better steric barrier and water solubility than chitosan and can have surface modifications because of additional carboxylic groups [15]. Because future wound dressings can potentially exhibit multiple functions, including accelerating blood clotting, inhibiting bacterial infections, and promoting full-layer wound regeneration, traditional wound dressings have structural and functional defects and cannot effectively promote wound healing [16,17]. CMC may promote the growth of keratinocytes and fibroblasts, increase the aggregation of red blood cells and platelet adhesion, and shorten the coagulation time [18,19]. However, it lacks the biological activity necessary for tissue regeneration engineering. Therefore, they are transformed into bioactive carbon quantum dots. In addition, wound healing is affected by many factors [4].

The healing of skin wounds is affected by the pH of the surrounding area [20]. A suitable weak acidic environment can promote skin metabolism and accelerate wound healing, and a lower pH can also inhibit the activity of some bacteria [21]. The optimal pH for the growth of S. aureus is 7.4. Citric acid (CA), an organic acid widely present in nature, can provide an acidic environment and inhibit bacterial activity. Moreover, adding an appropriate amount of elemental N to CDs can increase the output of the powder after the lyophilization of CDs [22]. Therefore, while preparing CDs in the present study, we used a one-step hydrothermal method to improve the production efficiency of the product—we added a small amount of urea to increase the rate of CD powder production.

In this study, CMC and CA were used as carbon sources, and a small amount of urea was added to improve the nanomaterials' powder yield and optical properties. The mass ratio of CMC, CA, and urea was 5:5:1. We analyzed the morphology, crystal structure, and fluorescence properties of the nanomaterials using ultraviolet (UV) absorption peaks, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), photoluminescence (PL), surface component elements, and surface functional groups. The prepared CDs were co-cultured with L929 cells in in vitro experiments to evaluate their ability to bioimage and promote fiber regeneration and determine the optimal concentration of CDs promoting fibroblast differentiation. To ensure the long-term non-toxicity of CDs to cells and tissues after practical application, we conducted in vitro antibacterial tests and in vivo animal experiments. This study provides a theoretical basis for applying biological CDs to promote tissue regeneration in vitro and in vivo and provides a new type of tissue regeneration nanomaterial.

CMC, CA, and urea were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd.. Dialysis bags (MWCO =1000 Da) were obtained from MYM Biological Technology Co., Ltd. Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) was purchased from Gibco (Life Technologies, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Fetal bovine serum was purchased from CellMax (Gansu, China). Streptomycin (100 mg·mL−1) and penicillin (100 U·mL−1) were purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific (Waltham, MA, USA). Cell Counting Kit 8 (CCK-8) and Calcein-AM/PI Double Staining Kit were purchased from Solarbio (Beijing, China) and Dalian Meilun Biotechnology Co., Ltd., respectively. L929 mouse fibroblasts were obtained from the laboratory of West China College of Stomatology, Sichuan University (Chengdu, China). The reagents used in this study were of analytical grade and were not subjected to further purification. Ultrapure deionized water was used for all the experiments.

CMC, CA, and urea were used as the carbon sources in a one-step hydrothermal method. In a classical experiment, 0.5 g of CMC and CA each and 0.1 g of urea were added to 40 mL of ultrapure water and then transferred to an 80 mL PPL-lined high-temperature reaction kettle under vacuum at 200 °C for 18 h. After the reaction was completed and the kettle was allowed to cool naturally to room temperature, the product was filtered using a 0.22 μm microporous filter membrane to obtain a CD solution. Next, the CD solution was dialyzed using a permeable membrane (MWCO =1 kDa) in ultrapure water for 36 h; the water was changed every 6–10 h. Finally, the purified CD solution was lyophilized in a vacuum lyophilizer to obtain CD powder. UV–vis spectroscopy was used to identify the absorption peaks of the CDs, and PL and UV–vis absorption spectra were used to describe the fluorescence characteristics of the materials. The CDs' composition and surface functional groups were examined using FTIR and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used to examine the dialyzed CDs and characterize their appearance and structure. The CD crystal structures were identified by X-ray diffraction (XRD). A zeta analysis of the surface charges of the materials was performed to assess their stability.

QY = 0.54 quinine sulfate (0.05 mol·L−1 of H2SO4) was used as the PL standard. QYs were calculated as follows.
Y u
=
Y s
×
F u
F s
×
A s
A u
×
Ƞ u 2
Ƞ s
2 .
Where Y is the QY, F is the integrated area of the fluorescence peak, Ƞ is the refractive index of the solvent, A is the absorbance, and s and u are the standard and test samples, respectively [23].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biocompatibility and cytofluorimetric imaging of CDs

ce:section.ce:para:
Exogenous WST-8 is converted to highly water-soluble orange formazan crystals by succinate dehydrogenase in the mitochondria of living cells, but dead cells lack this function. Therefore, we used the CCK-8 assay to detect the cytotoxicity of the CDs and their effect on cell proliferation. L929 cells were inoculated on 96-well plates with 1 × 104 cells per well and incubated in a thermostat for 24 h (37 °C, 5 % CO2). Then, the medium was replaced with 100 μL of DMEM containing different concentrations of CDs (0, 50, 100, 200, 400, and 800 μg·mL−1) for 1, 3, and 5 days (8 replicate wells were set up for each group). On the corresponding days, 10 μL of CCK-8 reagent was added to each well, and the absorbance at 450 nm was determined after the culture was incubated for 1.5 h using an enzymatic standard and a microplate spectrophotometer. Cell activity was calculated using the following equation.
Cell activity
%
=
OD
OD 0
× 100 %
OD0 was measured in the absence of CDs and OD in the presence of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Live/dead cell viability assay of L929
ce:section.ce:para: When L929 cells were in the logarithmic growth phase, 2 × 104 cells per well were inoculated on 24-well plates after counting and incubated in a thermostat for 24 h (37 °C, 5 % CO2). After this period, the medium was replaced with 500 μL of DMEM containing different concentrations of CDs (0, 50, 100, 200, 400, and 800 μg·mL−1) for 1, 3, and 5 days (8 replicate wells were set up for each group). On the corresponding days, the original medium was discarded, and the cells in the dish were washed three times with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS). Next, the cells were stained with AM/PI, incubated for 18 min, and then rinsed three times with PBS. Finally, the cells were observed under a fluorescence microscope.

When the L929 cells were in the logarithmic growth phase, 2 × 104 cells were inoculated in a special confocal culture dish after counting. After 24 h of incubation, the original medium was discarded, and two media with different concentrations of CDs (100 and 200 μg·mL−1) were used. After incubation for 12 h, the medium was discarded again, and the cells were washed with PBS thrice. Next, 1 mL of 4 % formaldehyde was added to the dish, and the cells were fixed for 15 min. After discarding the fixative, the cells were carefully washed with PBS thrice. Triton X-100 was added to the cells and then discarded after 3 min, followed by washing the cells with PBS three times. Then, the cells were stained with phalloidin for 30 min, and after discarding the stain, they were washed with PBS three times. Next, the cells were stained with 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI) for 10 min, and after discarding the stain, the cells were rinsed with PBS three times. All the above experiments were performed in the dark. Finally, confocal microscopy was used to observe the fluorescence imaging characteristics of the cells at different excitation wavelengths.

The plate counting method that counts the number of CFUs was adopted to determine the antibacterial performance of CDs. S. aureus experiments were divided into three groups: (1) S. aureus, (2) S. aureus + H2O2, and (3) S. aureus + CDs. Each group contained the same number of bacterial cells. The working concentrations of CDs were 100 and 200 μg·mL−1. The mixtures were incubated in a 37 °C shaker at 120 rpm for 30 min. Diluted mixtures of 100 μL were then coated on agar plates and cultured at 37 °C for 18 h. Finally, the relative bacterial activity was obtained by counting colonies.
Antibacterial rate % =
1 −
CFU
each group
CFU
control
× 100 %
CFU(control) is the CFU of the control group, and CFU(each group) is the CFU of the different treatment groups.
The mixed system of S. aureus and CDs (200 μg·mL−1) was incubated at 37 °C for 18 h, and the fluorescence images of CDs in bacteria were observed by CLSM.

After different treatments, the mixtures were centrifuged (5000 rpm) for 5 min, washed twice with PBS, and fixed with 2.5 % glutaric dialdehyde for 2.5 h. Subsequently, the bacteria were gradually dehydrated using 50 %, 70 %, 90 %, and 100 % ethanol. Finally, scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was used to observe the morphological changes in all the samples.

SD male mice (8 weeks, 300 ± 20 g) were purchased from the Animal Experimental Center of Shanxi Medical University (Taiyuan, Shanxi). All animal care and experimental procedures were performed under institutional standards for animal health and well-being and were approved by the Ethical Review Committee for Scientific Research of the Stomatology Hospital of Shanxi Medical University.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo wound healing evaluation and histology staining
ce:section.ce:para:
Four rounded wounds, approximately 2 cm in diameter, were symmetrically established on the back of the spine of each mouse. The mice were randomly divided into four groups: (1) control group, (2) CMC group, (3) 100 μg·mL−1 CD group, and (4) 200 μg·mL−1 CD group. The treatments were administered on days 1, 3, 5, and 7. Meanwhile, photographs of the wounds were taken on days 1, 5, 7, 10, and 14, and the size of the wounds was measured as follows: size = (
d 2
)
2
π , where d is the average length of the wound. After 14 days, the mice were sacrificed, and the major organs and granulation tissues of the wound were collected and fixed overnight with 4 % paraformaldehyde. After dehydration, the tissues were embedded in paraffin and analyzed by histopathological staining. Primary organs and granulation tissues were sliced using a paraffin slicer. We prepared tissue 4 μm-thick sections, and then dewaxed them and stained them with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E). The sections of the granulation tissue were also used for immunohistochemical staining with IL-6 and TGF-β.

In this study, CDs were synthesized using a one-step hydrothermal method with CMC, CA, and urea as the raw materials (Scheme 1 ). Without nitrogen doping, there was a significant decline in the powder production rate (Scheme 1b-c). As shown in Fig. 1a-d, the CD solution exhibited multicolor fluorescence under UV light (λExc = 365 nm) at different pH; however, the green fluorescence CDs (Fig. 1a) were selected according to the optimal pH for skin growth. Then, we further characterized the fluorescence properties of the selected CDs. The UV–vis absorption spectrum showed an absorption peak at 217 nm of the CDs solution, which is attributed to the electronic transition of the n orbital, and the absorption peak at 297 nm corresponds to the π → π*/n → π* transition of the C=C/C=O functional group on the surface of CDs (Fig. 1e) [24]. The CD solution exhibited an excitation-dependent PL feature (Fig. 2f). The CDs exhibited strong yellow-green fluorescence at a 466 nm excitation light, and the optimal emission wavelength was 536 nm (Fig. 1e-f). TEM revealed that the CDs were roughly spherical in shape, uniform in size, and well dispersed (Fig. 1g-i). Their size ranged from 1.4 to 19.28 nm with an average diameter of 2.13 nm. High-resolution TEM images (Fig. 1i) demonstrated that the lattice spacing of CDs was approximately 0.3 nm, which corresponds to the D-spacing of the graphene (100) plane [25,26]. The XRD pattern of the CDs is shown in Fig. 1j. CDs showed an obvious strong diffraction peak at 24.98°, corresponding to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon [27]. The (002) peak angle of graphitized carbon is about 26.5°, and a stronger intensity corresponds to a more complete crystal structure of the graphitized carbon [6,9]. The above results demonstrate that the prepared CD-graphitized materials had high crystallinity. As shown in Fig. 1k, the prepared CDs had a zeta potential of −20.1 mV (negative zeta potential) in water, and surface functional groups can lead to many emission traps to achieve high PL characteristics.

FTIR was used to analyze CDs' surface functional groups, as shown in Fig. 2a. The wide absorption peak of CDs at 3470–3150 comes from the stretching vibration of the N–H/O–H group [28]. The asymmetric stretching vibration at 2973 can be attributed to –CH2 [29]. The C=O/C=N stretching vibrations at 1590 indicated the presence of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, which facilitate dehydration and carbonization, respectively [30]. The peaks at 1412 and 1311 are attributed to CN bending vibrations [25]. The absorption peak at 1303 can be attributed to the stretching vibration of C–O–C. The absorption peaks at 709 and 613 can be attributed to CH stretching vibrations [31]. Moreover, the XPS spectrum confirmed that the CD surface contains many rich functional groups, such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, carbonyl, and N-containing functional groups. These results indicate that CDs are highly hydrophilic, water-soluble, and easy to utilize, indicating the scope for their future application. XPS showed that the main elements in the CDs were C, N, and O (Fig. 2b). High-resolution map of C1s (Fig. 2c) showed that there were three main types of C: C=O/C–C (284.8 eV), C–O/C–N (285.8 eV), and CO (288.6 eV) [32,33]. From the N1s spectrum shown in Fig. 2d, it can be observed that N was mainly present as NH (399.6 eV) and CN (400.5 eV) [34]. Further, the high-resolution spectrum of O1s in Fig. 2e demonstrates that the main composition of O was OH (531.6 eV) and CO (532.8 eV) [35]. In summary, the FTIR and XPS analyses were consistent, and the presence of numerous oxygen- and nitrogen-containing groups on the surface of the CDs provided strong evidence for their high water solubility and excellent bioactivity.

The QY of the green carbon quantum dots synthesized using the one-step hydrothermal method was 48.95 %. The abundant hydroxyl- and N-containing functional groups on the surfaces of CMC and CA increased the number of active sites on the CD surfaces so that the synthesized CDs had a stable fluorescence intensity. A previous study used chitosan and CMC as carbon sources to synthesize blue-luminous CDs, but their QY was only 9.0 % [36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of Mg-CDs on the proliferation of L929
ce:section.ce:para: The biosafety of CDs is crucial for their application in clinical treatments. Therefore, the cytotoxicity of CDs in L929 cells was investigated by measuring cell viability using the CCK-8 assay. As shown in Fig. 3a, all groups showed a decrease in cell viability within the normal range; however, the 200 μg·mL−1 CD group had increased cell viability compared with the control group. This may be because some cells do not adapt to the newly added material. These results indicate that the CDs have good biocompatibility with L929 cells without exhibiting cytotoxicity. The proliferative effect of CDs on L929 cells was detected using a CCK-8 kit. As shown in Fig. 3b, cell density increases significantly at 100 and 200 μg·mL−1 concentrations, which may be due to the close correlation between CMC in CDs and fibroblast proliferation and other functions [37]. As shown in Fig. 3c, the live/dead staining of cells also indicated that the CDs had no apparent cytotoxicity, suggesting they had good biocompatibility and were suitable for long-term cell growth. The proliferation of L929 cells facilitates the synthesis of collagen fibrin and extracellular matrix, which in turn promotes the replacement of the lost wound site with new fibers and capillaries in the initial stage of wound healing and the emergence of granulation tissue. Therefore, the proliferation of L929 cells contributes to the efficiency of wound healing. Although other concentrations of CDs did not show cytotoxicity as well, 100 and 200 μg·mL−1 were the most statistically significant; thus, they were selected as the safe concentrations for subsequent experiments.

To evaluate the effects of CD intake on cells and the distribution of CDs in cells, CDs, DAPI, and phalloidin were used, which emitted green, blue, and red fluorescence, respectively. The confocal imaging results of L929 cells were observed at 100 and 200 μg· mL−1 CD concentrations after 12 h. The morphology of L929 cells was normal, indicating that the CDs were not cytotoxic and were suitable for labeling living cells (Fig. 4 ). At the corresponding specific excitation wavelength, the cells emitted bright green fluorescence. Superimposed images of the bright and dark fields showed that the fluorescence emitted by the two concentrations of CDs almost covered the entire area of the cytoplasm, and the fluorescence intensity was stable. However, the 200 μg·mL−1 concentration enabled CDs to enter the nucleus more efficiently than the 100 μg·mL−1 concentration. In addition, we captured the dynamic moment when the cell ingested CDs, as indicated by the white arrow in Fig. 4j. These results imply that CDs can fluorescently label L929 cells. Based on this, CDs can be used as nanoreagents for biological imaging to observe the fluorescence in the nucleus and cytoplasm under a fluorescence microscope. Furthermore, the cell outline was clear in our images, which helps visualize dynamic changes in cell morphology through fluorescence imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro antibacterial performance of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: S. aureus is a main bacterial species responsible for oral and maxillofacial wound infections. Therefore, current oral and maxillofacial infection research focuses on inhibiting its growth and promoting oral and maxillofacial skin wound healing. H2O2 is commonly used for sterilizing and disinfecting skin wounds in clinical practice. In this study, we compared the antibacterial properties of H2O2 and CDs.

The inhibitory effect of CDs on S. aureus growth was evaluated using the plate counting method, as shown in Fig. 5a. Compared with the control group, the number of bacteria in the H2O2 group was slightly reduced, but this number was higher than the number of bacteria in the CD group; thus, the inhibition of bacterial growth was more prominent in the CD group (Fig. 5b). The bactericidal rate of the 200 μg·mL−1 CD group for S. aureus was 93.59 %, higher than that of the H2O2 group (23.82 %). This phenomenon may be because H2O2 inhibits bacterial growth only at the initial stage of contact with the bacteria. In contrast, the abundant functional groups on the surface of CDs can produce a more lasting inhibitory effect on bacterial growth. In addition, the optimal pH for the growth of S. aureus is 7.4, whereas the pH of the CD solution was 6–7, which may interfere with the growth of bacteria. Other studies have shown that CDs with highly positive charges can easily bind to negatively charged bacteria and directly destroy bacterial cell membranes via electrostatic interactions. For example, the quaternized CDs synthesized by Zhao et al. using a one-step hydrothermal method showed satisfactory antibacterial activity against gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria [38]. Supercationic CDs synthesized by Jian et al. showed lasting inhibition of non-multidrug-resistant and multidrug-resistant bacteria [39]. However, to the best of our knowledge, the surface charge is not the key factor influencing the antibacterial performance of CDs.

To understand the cellular mechanism of the antibacterial action of CDs, we investigated the untake of these materials into baceterals by utilizing CDs intrinsic fluorescence. The mixed system of S. aureus and CDs (200 μg·mL−1) was incubated at 37 °C for 12 h, and the fluorescence images of CDs in bacteria were clearly visible under excitation light (Fig. 5c). Then, we further observed the adsorption of CDs to bacteria by SEM. As shown in Fig. 5d (red circle), we observed that CDs adsorbed on the surface of bacteria and had an effect on bacteria. Finally, the morphology of bacteria was observed by SEM. As shown in Fig. 5e, S. aureus in control generally exhibited its complete and original appearance, indicating that the bacteria were nearly undamaged and in a relatively intact survival state. In the H2O2 group, the bacteria exhibited adhesion, but their morphology remained intact. However, in both the CD concentration groups, the structure of the bacteria was significantly damaged; the bacteria no longer had a spherical appearance (orange arrow in Fig. 5e), and their mycelia were significantly wrinkled and ruptured. These results confirmed that the CDs synthesized in this study contained many –OH and CO functional groups. Although these negatively charged CDs may not exhibit strong bacterial adhesion, they can still coat the outer surface of bacteria and become indirectly toxic to bacterial cells, owing to the biological protection of the growth medium, which is consistent with the results of previous studies on the antibacterial mechanism of negatively charged carbon dots [40]. Therefore, adding CDs can adsorb to the surface of the bacteria and prevent bacteria from proliferating or consuming nutrients, thus destroying the bacterial cell wall and/or membrane (antibacterial mechanism in Fig. 5f), which is the mechanism of bacterial death caused by membrane damage [41,42].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Wound antibacterial and healing efficacy of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Encouraged by the in vitro antibacterial properties of CDs, we evaluated the role of CDs in wound healing and antibacterial activity using a bacterial-infected skin wound model. Before this, we evaluated the cytotoxicity of CDs in L929 cells (Figs. 3, 4). We observed that cell viability was within the normal range even at a dose of 200 μg·mL−1, indicating good biocompatibility of CDs. Wound healing usually occurs chronologically and is a complex process that involves blood coagulation, inflammation, new tissue formation, and tissue remodeling [43].

First, four round wounds of about 2 cm in diameter were established on the back of each male SD rat with the spine as the center, and the infected wound model was constructed by injecting 50 μL S. aureus into the back. The bacterial density was 1.0 × 108 CFU·mL−1. The rats were divided into four groups, (1) control group, (2) H2O2 group, (3) 100 μg·mL−1 CD group, and (4) 200 μg·mL−1 CD group. After photographing the wound area on different days, we obtained a graph showing the trends in wound healing and blood scab changes in the other groups infected with S. aureus. Bacterial deposition and pyogenic development were observed in each group before treatment on day 1. On the 5th day, compared with the control group, the CD groups exhibited a significantly reduced purulent condition, and the wound had entered the scab stage, indicating the proliferation and differentiation of new cells and the formation of granulation tissue. Compared with the H2O2 group, there was obvious inflammation and local exudation on the CD surfaces, indicating that the stage of hemostasis and inflammation of the wound was over; thus, a moderately acidic pH environment is more suitable for wound healing than a neutral environment. Notably, the wounds of the CD groups healed after 14 days, and the wounds of the 200 μg·mL−1 CD group were completely healed. However, wound healing was not observed in the control and H2O2 groups, and this result indicated that our synthesized CDs have a wound healing-promoting effect. It is worth noting that the changes in the CD and H2O2 groups were most obvious during 1–7 days after the establishment of wounds.

In addition, we collected the dermal tissues around the wound of each group on the 14th day. We conducted a pathological analysis to examine the microscopic changes in the wound-healing process. The H&E staining images (Fig. 5e) show that new surface tissue appeared in the damaged tissue area, and a large number of fibroblasts was observed, which is consistent with the fact that damaged tissues are replaced by a large amount of fibrous connective tissue during repair. Many disordered collagen fibers were observed in the H2O2 group, which mainly comprised inflammatory cells (lymphocytes and neutrophils, marked with yellow arrows), and neovasculature was rare. Some studies have confirmed that H2O2 has a strong bactericidal effect but delays wound healing, consistent with our research results [44]. In the control group, many inflammatory cells gathered in the collagen fibers, and the surface tissue was not whole, indicating severe wound infection and inflammation. In contrast, the CD groups showed a completely keratinized epidermis and regular collagen fibers within the dermis, almost no inflammatory cells, numerous collagen fiber bundles, new capillaries (marked by red arrows), and regenerated hair follicles (marked by black arrows), suggesting that CDs had better antibacterial effects and improved wound healing. These results demonstrate that a CD-based treatment platform can considerably promote wound healing.

Furthermore, to evaluate the quality of wound healing and to examine the intrinsic effects of the wound healing process, immunohistochemical (IHC) staining experiments were performed. The inflammatory response caused by a wound infection is one of the main factors affecting wound healing. Thus, a typical pro-inflammatory factor, IL-6, was selected, and its expression in the wounds of rats in each group was measured using IHC staining to assess the infection prevention efficiency of CDs. As shown in Fig. 6d-e, a densely distributed yellowish-brown color with varying shades was detected in the control and H2O2 groups, indicating a high inflammation level with a high level of IL-6 expression and severe infection. In contrast, the range of positivity in the CD group was more dispersed and yellowish, indicating a weaker degree of positivity and minimal infection. In addition, the expression of TGF-β, which promotes cell growth and differentiation and is an important cellular mediator of wound healing, was also assessed using IHC experiments. In the control and H2O2 groups, as shown in Fig. 6d-e, the brown color range was less and dispersed, indicating a weak degree of positivity and a low level of TGF-β expression. In contrast, in the CD group, the brown color was densely distributed with a yellowish-brown color, indicating a higher level of TGF-β expression relative to the control and H2O2 groups. This indicated that CDs retained the original performance of CMC, which can increase the levels of TGF-β [45,46] and accelerate wound healing at the initial stage of the wound. Moreover, the weak acidic pH provided a favorable growth environment. In summary, these results denoted that the CDs-mediated therapeutic platform was potent to promote fibroblast generation and healing of infected wounds.

In conclusion, we demonstrated the formation of weakly acidic CDs that can be used for in vitro experiments and wound healing efficiently and safely. The prepared CDs exhibited satisfactory fluorescence properties and can be used for real-time labeling. In vitro, antibacterial experiments showed that CDs have efficient antibacterial properties. In addition, CDs contribute to fibroblast proliferation, neointima formation, and collagen deposition, leading to the healing of bacterial-infected wounds. Almost complete wound healing was achieved in the in vivo treatments. More importantly, ultrasmall CDs exhibit good biocompatibility and do not cause substantial inflammation or pathological damage to normal tissues. Overall, this work provides a new and feasible way to prepare carbon quantum dots with antimicrobial activity and to evaluate carbon quantum dots for skin wound healing, expanding a new development direction for the preparation of novel carbon quantum dots.

Mingrui Zong: Methodology, Investigation, Visualization, Writing an original draft. Zheyuan Zhang: Visualization, Data curation. Xiao Ning: Investigation, Visualization. Huaiyi Cheng: Data curation. Yifan Zhao, Jianing Ren: Investigation. Yingyu Liu, Ran Zhang, Jiayu Cui: Data curation. Yuxi Hou: Methodology. Bing Li: Project administration, Funding acquisition. Xiuping Wu: Resources, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

This research was funded by the Shanxi Province Science and Technology Innovation Key Talent Team (202204051002034), the Key Research and Development Plan of Shanxi Province (202102130501002), the key country-specific science and cooperation projects of the Shanxi Provincial Science and Technology Department (202204041101004), and the central guidance for local science and technology development funds (YDZJSX2022C029 and YDZJSX2021C011).

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
